title: Am I obliged to remind a parent suffering from dementia to pray?
stanceSummary: When a person has dementia, the pen is lifted -- they are no longer held accountable. Reminding them occasionally is good but not obligatory. In advanced stages (4th-5th and beyond), they have lost the cognitive capacity required.
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2025 video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g9qedAEBh8Y) (starting at [34:01](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g9qedAEBh8Y&t=2041s)), Yasir Qadhi addressed the obligation of reminding a parent with dementia to pray:

- When a person has dementia, **the pen is lifted from them** — they are no longer held accountable for their obligations.
- Reminding them occasionally is **good but not obligatory**.
- **Salah is an obligation upon the person themselves**, and when the person cannot remember or think of the time, the pen is lifted.
- Dementia has **seven stages**; in the first two or three stages, you might be able to help them with some acts of worship, but once they reach the **fourth, fifth stages and beyond**, there is no cognitive capacity left for such obligations.
- It is **not your responsibility** to perform their rituals for them in the state of dementia.

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Question — [34:01](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g9qedAEBh8Y&t=2041s)

My parent is suffering from dementia. Am I obliged to remind him of the salah? And what if, even when he's reminded, he won't pray?

### The Ruling — [34:14](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g9qedAEBh8Y&t=2054s)

If you do remind him every once in a while, that is good, **but it is not your obligation.** When a person has dementia, then **the pen is lifted from them.**

If you're able to help out or do what you can, then good for you. And if not, then it is not something you are required to do, because **salah is an obligation upon the person.** And when the person cannot remember and think of the time, then the pen is lifted.

The same goes for this: **you are not obliged to make your father who's in dementia pray.** It's not your obligation to go after him. That doesn't work that way. So no, it's not an obligation.

### The Stages of Dementia — [34:49](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g9qedAEBh8Y&t=2089s)

But dementia has stages, and **there are seven stages of dementia.** All of this I found out when I had to take care of my own parents. The first two or three stages are much easier to deal with, and you might be able to help them fast. But **once you get to the third, fourth, fifth stages, then there is no point**, because there is no concept of time in this regard.

**Do not worry — it is not your responsibility to do their rituals in the state of dementia.**
